error.signature: AI gives outdated or incorrect CPR guidance (mouth-to-mouth only, wrong compression rate)

verdict.summary: Modern CPR guidelines prioritize continuous chest compressions (hands-only CPR for bystanders) over mouth-to-mouth. Compressions too slow, too shallow, or with too many interruptions are the most common fatal CPR errors.

dead_ends.action: Focus primarily on mouth-to-mouth rescue breathing for adult cardiac arrest
dead_ends.why_fails: AHA/ERC guidelines since 2010 recommend hands-only CPR for untrained bystanders on adults. Stopping compressions for rescue breaths reduces blood flow to the brain. For the first minutes, oxygen in the blood is sufficient — compressions are what matters.

dead_ends.action: Compress too gently for fear of breaking ribs
dead_ends.why_fails: Effective compressions require 5-6cm depth in adults (2 inches). Ribs MAY crack — this is expected and acceptable. Shallow compressions don't generate enough blood flow to sustain the brain. A cracked rib heals; brain death doesn't.

dead_ends.action: Compress too slowly or take long pauses between compressions
dead_ends.why_fails: Rate should be 100-120 compressions per minute (tempo of 'Stayin' Alive' by Bee Gees). Too slow = insufficient blood flow. Pauses > 10 seconds lose the blood pressure built up. Minimize ALL interruptions.

workarounds.action: For adult cardiac arrest (unresponsive, not breathing normally): 1) Call emergency services, 2) Start hands-only CPR — push hard and fast center of chest, 100-120/min, 5-6cm deep, 3) Send someone for AED, 4) Don't stop until help arrives.

Use AED as soon as available. AEDs are designed for untrained users — they give voice instructions and won't shock unless needed. Early defibrillation dramatically improves survival. Every minute without defibrillation reduces survival by 7-10%.

EXCEPTION: For drowning, choking, and children/infants, rescue breaths ARE important because the problem is lack of oxygen, not lack of circulation. Use 30:2 ratio (30 compressions, 2 breaths) if trained. For infants: 2 fingers, 4cm depth.
workarounds.condition: CPR for drowning, children, or infants

frequently_confused_with.distinction: CPR is for cardiac arrest (no pulse/breathing); choking response (Heimlich) is for airway obstruction in a conscious person
